pattern: A sparse spreadsheet only needs to remember the cells someone actually touched; anything never set defaults to zero, so a plain map from cell name to value is enough to evaluate a two-operand sum formula.
hints: There is no need to allocate a full rows-by-26 grid up front — most cells will never be written, so only the ones that were explicitly set need to occupy any storage.
A formula is always exactly two operands joined by a plus sign after the leading '='; splitting on that single '+' separates the two operand tokens cleanly.

Each operand token is either a cell reference (a capital letter followed by digits) or a bare non-negative integer literal — decide which by looking at the token's first character, and treat any cell that was never set (or was reset) as the value zero.

constraints.note: a capital letter A-Z followed by a 1-indexed row number <= rows
always "=X+Y" where X and Y are each either a cell reference or a non-negative integer literal <= 100000

edge_inputs.description: sample sequence: literal-only formula, cell references, and a reset back to zero
a formula built from two plain integer literals, with no cells involved at all
a cell referenced before ever being set must read as zero, not raise an error
resetCell on a cell that was never set is a harmless no-op
a cell at the highest valid row for this sheet
values at the constraint extremes: zero and the maximum cell value
setting the same cell twice keeps only the latest value, it does not accumulate
a multi-digit row number in a cell reference must be parsed as one cell, not split apart

stress.description: random setCell/resetCell/getValue traffic over a handful of cells and formulas
stress.note: small 5-column cell alphabet makes repeated set/reset/read collisions on the same cells likely
